coredata.dc:title: Poly-(2,6-diaminopyridine) nanoparticles enhanced fluorescent system: An “on–off” fluorescent switch triggered by fluorescent sensing platform
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Fang
Yang, Siwei
coredata.dc:description: An oxidation–reduction fluorescent switch system is prepared based on low-cost and easily prepared poly-(2,6-diaminopyridine) (PdAP) nanoparticles (NPs) and bovine hemoglobin (Hb). As a fluorescent sensing platform, the PdAP NPs can enhance the PL intensity of Hb markedly. The system is triggered by the redox process of PdAP NPs catalyzed by Hb. This switch system is highly sensitive and selective with high signal-to-noise ratio and can be used as a fluorescent probe for vitamin C and H2O2 (ppb level). All geometries and energies were investigated by generalized gradient approximation of density functional theory.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Conducting polymer
Photoluminescence
Fluorescent probe
Redox

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Working mechanism of oxidation–reduction fluorescence switching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the molecular structure of PdAP in oxidation state and reduction state.
Schematic diagram illustrating the mechanism of the oxidation–reduction fluorescence switch.
(a) Low and high magnification SEM images of the products thus formed. (b) Corresponding dot size distribution histograms. (c) Raman spectra of the products. (d) FTIR spectra of the products.

(a) Fluorescence excitation spectra and emission spectra of Hb without and with the presence of PdAP, respectively. (b) Fluorescence response of PdAP toward a series of analytes. The intensities of each analyte (F) in the presence of PdAP were compared with fluorescence intensities of each analyte without the presence of PdAP (F 0). (1) alanine (Ala), (2) tryptophan (Trp), (3) tyrosine (Tyr), (4) arginine (Arg), (5) spartic acid (Asp), (6) serine (Ser), (7) glutamic acid (Glu), (8) phenylalanine (Phe), (9) proline (Pro), (10) glycine (Gly), (11) lysine (Lys), (12) isoleucine (Ile), (13) cystine (Met), (14) cysteine (Cys), (15) glutathione (Gsh), (16), thiophenol, (17) mercaptoethanol, (18) dodecyl mercaptan, (19) Hb, (20) yeast RNA. (c) PL intensityof Hb–PdAP complex at 386nm upon different concentrations of PdAP. The insert shows the linear relation of the fluorescent intensity of the PdAP concentration (1×10−9–2×10−7 gL−1). (d) The interference experiment of Hb–PdAP complex. The intensities of Hb (F) in the presence of distractions were compared with fluorescence intensities of each analyte without the presence of distractions (F 0). (1) Ala, (2) Trp, (3) Tyr, (4) Arg, (5) Asp, (6) Ser, (7) Glu, (8) Gly, (9) Lys, (10) Phe, (11) Pro, (12) Ile, (13) Met, (14) Cys, (15) Gsh, (16) vitamine A (VA), (17) vitamine B2 (VB2), (18) vitamine E (VE), (19) vitamine D (VD), (20) vitamine K (VK), (21) vitamine B12 (VB12), and (22) inositol. Excitation at 272nm; in PBS buffer solution (pH 7.26) at 37°C.

(a) PL intensity of switch system at “off” state upon different concentrations of VC in the presence of PdAP. The insert shows Stern–Volmer plot for the quenching of Hbs PL by VC (excitation at 284nm; F 0 and F are Hbs PL intensities at 386nm in the absence and presence of VC, respectively). (b) PL intensity of switch system at “on” state upon different concentrations of H2O2 in the presence of PdAP (excitation at 272nm; in PBS buffer solution (pH 7.26) at 37°C).

(a) PL intensity of switch system upon different concentrations of VC and H2O2 in the presence of PdAP. (b) Color-coded contour plot of PL intensity of switch system as a function of VC and H2O2 in the presence of PdAP (excitation at 272nm; in PBS buffer solution (pH 7.26) at 37°C).

(a) PL intensity of switch system (1) reference group: Hb+PdAP+VC+H2O2, the switch system reduced by (2) citric acid, (3) NaBH4, (4) N2H4, (5) VC, (6) aniline, (7) Na2S2O4, and (8) Na2SO3. (b) PL intensity of switch system (1) reference group: Hb+PdAP+VC, the switch system oxidized by (2) KMnO4, (3) NaClO, (4) (NH4)2S2O8, (5) H2O2, (6) O2, (7) K2Cr2O7, and (8) HNO3 (excitation at 272nm; in PBS buffer solution (pH 7.26) at 37°C).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectrums and (b) fluorescence intensity of this fluorescent switch system at “on” or “off” state in 15 redox cycles (excitation at 272nm; in PBS buffer solution (pH 7.26) at 37°C).
PL intensity of Hb+VC, Hb+H2O2, Hb+PdAP+VC and Hb+PdAP+H2O2 (excitation at 272nm; in PBS buffer solution (pH 7.26 at 37°C).
(a) Situ UV–vis spectrums of PdAP, PdAP+VC and PdAP+Hb+VC. (b) Cyclic voltammograms of PdAP and Hb/PdAP.
The molecular structure of all kinds of molecules in calculation.
The molecular configurations and electric cloud distribution of HOMO–LUMO of all molecules.
The band gap (BG, eV) and the energy levels of molecular orbitals (HOMO, LUMO).

head.ce:title: Poly-(2,6-diaminopyridine) nanoparticles enhanced fluorescent system: An “on–off” fluorescent switch triggered by fluorescent sensing platform

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Functional Materials for Informatics, Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology, Chinese Academy of Science, Shanghai 20050, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Functional Materials for Informatics
Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An oxidation–reduction fluorescent switch system is prepared based on low-cost and easily prepared poly-(2,6-diaminopyridine) (PdAP) nanoparticles (NPs) and bovine hemoglobin (Hb). As a fluorescent sensing platform, the PdAP NPs can enhance the PL intensity of Hb markedly. The system is triggered by the redox process of PdAP NPs catalyzed by Hb. This switch system is highly sensitive and selective with high signal-to-noise ratio and can be used as a fluorescent probe for vitamin C and H2O2 (ppb level). All geometries and energies were investigated by generalized gradient approximation of density functional theory.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, much attention has been focused on the development of fluorescent switches in supramolecular chemistry [1–9]. A great number of fluorescent dyes have been applied to construct the fluorescent switch system with high sensibility [10–18]. Li et al. had synthesized an “off–on–off” switch based on 2,3,15,16-tetrakis(pyridin-2-yl)-7,8,10,11,20,21,23,24 octahydro [1,4,7,10,13,16]-hexaoxacyclooctadecino[2,3-g:11,12-g′]diquinoxaline which could be used as a selective fluorescent sensor for Zn2+ [19]. Sánchez et al. had synthesized a fluorescent molecular switch that could be light- and redox-controlled [20]. However, most dyes suffer from complex synthesis, and what is worse, they are found toxic to biological tissues [21–24]. Generally, the fluorescent switches are triggered by supramolecular interactions between fluorescent molecules and ions/molecules [20–26]. Therefore, the changes of fluorescent molecular structure in “on–off” progress always result in poor stability of the switch systems, which to some extent hinder their practical application [19,21,23–32]. Furthermore, as a very important physiological progress, the redox progress holds profound significance in physiology [33,34]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there are few reports on fluorescent switch triggered by redox progress of sensing platform (like polymer structures) [33–39].

Owing to their well-defined nature and the combination of the positive mechanical and physical properties of polymeric species, conducting polymers (CPs) had attracted increasing attention since the polyacetylene was first synthesized in 1977 [40]. A variety of CPs such as polyaniline (PANI), polythiophene (PTH), polyparaphenylene (PPP), polypyridine (PPY) were synthesized [41–45]. These polymers with conjugated structure always have good properties in charge transportation and hydrophilic due to the hybrid atoms (such as nitrogen and sulfur) in conjugated structure [46–48]. PANI and PPY have been widely used in fluorescent probes [49–54]. Sun et al. synthesized various CP NPs such as PANI, poly(o-phenylenediamine) (PoPD), poly(m-phenylenediamine) (PmPD), Ag@PmPD, poly(p-phenylenediamine) (PpPD) and polynaphthylamine which were applied in the fluorescence detection of nucleic acid [55–59]. The results showed that these NPs could absorb and quench dye-labeled single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) very effectively. As sensitive fluorescent sensing platforms, these CP NPs played an important role in the fluorescence detection progress [58,60]. What is more, our previous reports have revealed that the PpPD nanofibers show high-efficiency effects in enhancing the fluorescence intensity of thiols through the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [61–63]. 1H NMR study shows that the strong hydrogen bond between PpPD and thiols is the possible media of FRET progress. The theoretical calculated results further conform the obtained data [62,63]. These reports show that the CPs have favorable property and have great potential application in fluorescent switch or logic gate [55–58].

In this paper, a simple and straightforward strategy was designed to fabricate a sensitive and selective oxidation–reduction fluorescent switch system based on poly-(2,6-diaminopyridine) (PdAP) NPs and bovine hemoglobin (Hb). This switch system is triggered by the Hb-catalyzed redox progress of PdAP, which also increase the PL intensity of Hb effectively through FRET progress. The operating state is “off” when vitamin C (VC) is added in this system; and in the state of “on” when PdAP NPs is oxidized by H2O2. Therefore, this switch system could be used as a sensitive and selective probe (ppb level) for VC and H2O2 with high stability. Moreover, all geometries and energies were investigated by generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of density functional theory (DFT).

(NH4)2S2O8 (APS), 2,6-diaminopyridine (dAP), Hb, H2O2 (30wt%), and VC were purchased from Alfa Aesar. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. The water used throughout all experiments was purified through a Millipore system.

The preparation of PdAP NPs was as follows: 1.5g dAP was dissolved in 10mL deionized water with the aid of ultrasound. After that, the mixture was maintained at ice–water bath for 0.5h. Then 6mL of APS (1.67M) aqueous solution was added to the above mixture to initiate the oxidative polymerization. The resulting solution was stirred for 0.5min to ensure complete mixing and then the reaction was allowed to proceed without agitation for 12h at 10°C. The brown product with yield of 86% was collected by centrifugation, washed two times with distilled water and then dried in vacuum. The resulting precipitate was then dispersed in water for further characterization and use.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements were made on a JSM-6510 (Rigaku, Japan) scanning electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 30kV. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum was measured on a Bruker Vector-22 FT-IR spectrometer from 4000 to 500cm−1 at room temperature. Raman spectrum was obtained on a J-Y T64000 Raman spectrometer with 514.5nm wavelength incident laser light. UV–vis spectra were obtained on a UV5800 Spectrophotometer.

The fluorescence emission spectra of Hb in the absence and presence of PdAP, VC and H2O2 were recorded on a PerkinElmer LS55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Instruments, U.K). The excitation wavelength was 284nm and the narrow deteriorates of excitation and emission filters were 5nm. Fluorescence quantum yields were determined for highly diluted solutions of the Hb (2×10−8 gL−1), Hb+PdAP (Hb: 2×10−8 gL−1, PdAP: 5×10−6 gL−1), Hb+PdAP+VC (Hb: 2×10−8 gL−1, PdAP: 5×10−6 gL−1, VC: 2×10−8 M) and Hb+PdAP+VC+H2O2 (Hb: 2×10−8 gL−1, PdAP: 5×10−6 gL−1, VC: 2×10−8 M, and H2O2: 2×10−8 M), and they were measured relative to rhodamine B in H2O (Φf
=1).

Electrochemical measurement was performed in a conventional three electrode cell using a CHI 660B electrochemical work station. A conventional three-electrode cell was used, including a glassy carbon (GC) disk (3mm in diameter), coated with modifier, as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and Pt wire as the counter electrode. The catalytic performance of Hb was determined by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in PBS solution (pH 7.26) at 37°C. To fabricate a working electrode, 1mg of modifier powder (pure PdAP or Hb/PdAP composite) was dispersed in 10mL water with ultrasonication for 30min to form a homogeneous suspension. Then 5μL of the resulting suspension was carefully pipetted onto the GC electrode surface, and the coating was dried at room temperature for 1h. The Hb/PdAP composite was prepared as follows: 10mg PdAP was dispersed in 100mL of 10mgL−1 Hb aqueous solution with ultrasonication for 30min at 37°C. The resulting solution was stirred for 6h and the product was collected by centrifugation, washed two times with distilled water and then dried in vacuum.

Standard DFT were performed using the DMol3 module [64]. All geometries and energies were calculated with the generalized gradient approximation (GGA), using the functional PW91 in combination with the double numerical polarized (DNP) basis set [65–67]. All geometries were relaxed in three directions of space, based on the geometry optimization criterion (RMS force of 0.002au/Å and RMS displacement of 0.005Å). The calculation of total energy and electronic structure was followed by the geometry optimization with SCF tolerance of 1×10−5 au. A Fermi smearing of 0.005Ha. The quality mesh size for numerical integration was chosen. The unrestricted approach was applied that the low-spin state, that is, singlet for systems with even-number electrons and doublet for systems with odd-number electrons. These low-spin states were in general energetically preferable.

Fig. 1 a shows the typical SEM images of the products thus formed, which clearly indicate that the products exclusively consisted of a large amount of nanoparticles. The high-magnification of SEM image shown in the inset shows the smooth surface of the PdAP NPs. Furthermore, the diameters of such NPs are range from 250 to 350nm (Fig. 1b).

Raman spectra are used to analyze the molecular structure of PdAP (Fig. 1c). The peak at 1368cm−1 is assigned to the pyridine ring. The bands at 1562 and 1597cm−1 are ascribed to the CC deformation of pyridine rings and quinoid rings, respectively [68–70]. The FT-IR spectrum of PdAP is shown in Fig. 1d. The broad peaks at 3189 and 3321cm−1 correspond to NH2 symmetric stretching mode and imply the presence of secondary amino groups of pyridine ring. The peak at 2989cm−1 is assigned to CH stretching of pyridine ring [55,71]. The CC and CN stretching vibrations of phenazine ring exhibit a strong peak at 1645cm−1 [56,72]. The peaks at 1387 and 1499cm−1 are associated with the CC and CN stretching of pyridine ring [55,73]. The peaks at 1289 and 1270cm−1 are associated with the CH stretching in the pyridine units [61,74–77]. What is more, the strong peak at 1030cm−1 corresponding to the CH deformation vibration of adjacent phenazine ring H’s [61,78]. Moreover, the peaks at 816 and 763cm−1 are assigned to CH deformation vibration of 2,5,6-trisubstituted pyridine ring and ring deformation vibration of phenazine ring, respectively [62,63]. The XRD patterns, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms, 1H NMR spectra and MALDI-TOF-MASS spectrum of PdAP further illustrates the structure of the products (Fig. S1–3, S16).

Fig. 2 a shows the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Hb in the absence and presence of PdAP, respectively. It is seen that the PL intensity at 323nm can be slightly enhanced by PdAP. However, an obvious enhancement (approximately 90-fold) in intensity can be observed at 386nm (Fig. S4 and S5). This can be attributed to the FRET procedure between Hb and the π-conjugation PdAP [79–86]. Therefore, we proposed a sensing platform toward Hb based on the fluorescence enhancement principle of PdAP NPs. The selectivity and specificity toward Hb are also been demonstrated. Fig. 2b indicates that PdAP does not give any observable enhancement for many biomolecules such as: alanine (Ala), tryptophan (Trp), tyrosine (Tyr), arginine (Arg), aspartic acid (Asp), serine (Ser), glutamic acid (Glu), phenylalanine (Phe), proline (Pro), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), Isoleucine (Ile), methionine (Met), and yeast RNA. The PL intensity of thiols has been slighty enhanced by PdAP. The reasons can be attributed to the hydrogen bonds to between thiols and PdAP [61–63,87,88].

For sensitivity study, different concentrations of Hb in the range of 1×10−12–5×10−7 gL−1 are investigated. Fig. 2c shows a gradual increase in PL intensity at 386nm with increased Hb concentration, revealing that the sensing system is sensitive to Hb. The correlation coefficients (r 2) is 0.99826 for determining Hb over the linear concentration range of 1×10−12–2×10−7 gL−1. The detection limit is estimated to be 2×10−13 gL−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3.

Besides sensitivity and selectivity, anti-interference is another important parameter to evaluate the performance of the sensing system. Therefore, we examined the PL intensity changes in the presence of interfering substances under the same conditions as shown in Fig. 2d. Obviously, no significant change is observed by adding other interfering substances into this sensing system. The outstanding selectivity and specificity can be probably attributed to that Hb has a stronger affinity toward the PdAP than other biomolecules. The PL intensity of this mixture in different pH and temperature are shown in Fig. S6 and S7, respectively.

The dramatic quenching behavior in PL intensity of Hb can be observed when VC was added into this system (Hb: 2×10−8 gL−1, PdAP: 5×10−6 gL−1, Fig. 3 a and S8). It is obvious that VC can strongly quench intrinsic fluorescence of Hb and the fluorescent switch is in the “off” state. The fluorescence quenching data follows the Stern–Volmer equation, via either a dynamic or a static mechanism: [89–91]
(1)
F
0
F − 1
=
K
s v
c
where K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, c is the analyte (VC) concentration and F 0, F are PL intensities of Hb at 386nm in the absence and presence of VC, respectively. The Stern–Volmer plot shown in Fig. 3b insert which fit a linear Stern–Volmer equation in the concentration range of 3×10−8–10×10−8 M and the quenching mechanism is static quenching. The correlation coefficients (r 2) and K sv are 0.9917 and 0.2047 determining VC over the linear concentration range of 3×10−8–10×10−8 M, respectively. The detection limit is estimated to be 5×10−9 M at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The observation may be attributed to the reduced status of PdAP initiated by reducing action of VC. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of the switch system may be recovered again when the PdAP is in oxidized state. Indeed, when adding a mild oxidant, H2O2, the switch system is oxidized by H2O2, which results in the fluorescence recovery of PdAP and fluorescent switch is in the “on” state (Fig. 3b and S9).

Fig. 4 a shows the PL intensity of switch system upon different concentrations of VC and H2O2 in the presence of PdAP. It is clear that the surface can be divided into the two regions: one corresponds to the “on” state, and the other corresponds to the “off” state. The threshold value of “on” state can be set to 500. And the threshold value of “off” state can be set to 200. Fig. 4b gives a color-coded contour plot of the slope values for this switch system. Obviously, the addition proportion of VC and H2O2 has a great influence on this switch’s states. When the molar ratio of VC and H2O2 is less than 0.8, the switch is “off”. The switch is in “on” state when the molar ratio higher than 1.25. The results indicate this system has high signal-noise ratio [92–95].

To further investigate the redox-controlled operation of this switch, it was subjected to repetitive catalytic oxidation and reduction while its fluorescence response was monitored. The results obtained are shown in Fig. 6 . The amplitude of the fluorescence changes measured remains nearly constant upon 12 redox cycles, which demonstrates the robustness and reproducibility of the redox-controlled operation of the switch system (Fig. S15)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working mechanism of oxidation–reduction fluorescence switching
ce:section.ce:para: In order to get a profound comprehension of the “on–off” progress, an investigation was performed in which redox system constructed the switch system firstly. Fig. 7 shows the PL intensity of Hb in different mixtures. In the presence of PdAP, the PL intensity decreased dramatically when VC was added into this system. However, no obvious change can be observed for PL intensity in the mixture of Hb and VC without PdAP. What is more, the H2O2 cannot change the PL intensity of Hb in the absence of PdAP. This indicates the PL intensity of Hb depends on the redox progress of PdAP.

The changes of molecular structure of PdAP in “on–off” progress are represented directly by in situ UV–vis spectroscopy. Fig. 8 a shows the ultraviolet spectrums of PdAP, PdAP+VC and PdAP+Hb+VC. The UV–vis spectrum of PdAP showed that there is a characteristic absorption peak centred at 353cm. This peak could be assigned to the π–π* transition of the π-conjugation systems [54–57,61–63]. When the VC was added in the PdAP, the absorption peak does not shift. This indicated that the molecular structure of PdAP did not change when the VC was added in the PdAP. However, a dramatic spectral shift is observed when VC was added in the presence of Hb. It is clear that the absorption peak was blue shifted to 316nm which reflects the structure change of π-conjugation systems in PdAP. The band gap of PdAP is broadened (0.4eV) and PdAP is reduced when VC is added in this system in the presence of Hb. Therefore, this switch system is triggered by the redox progress of PdAP with Hb as catalyst. The structure change of π-conjugation systems in PdAP is the key factor of the working mechanism in this switch system. The 1H NMR spectra also show the increasing of amino-group proportion which indicates this structure changes (Fig. S17–20).

The theoretic calculation of PdAP is performed for deeply understand the structure and conjugation changes of PdAP in redox progress. The previous reports confirmed that the CPs which was synthesized by polymerizing the mixtures of oligomers in the presence of APS act as initiator (Fig. S16–18) [38–45,96]. Considering this issue, we chose 3 kinds of oligomers (A1, B1 and C1) of PdAP as object. Moreover, the possible structures of PdAP (A2, B2, B3 and C2-5) which were reduced by VC were also considered (Fig. 9 ).

The band gap (BG, eV) and the energy levels of molecular orbitals (HOMO, LUMO) are shown in Fig. 11 and Table S2. It is obvious that the BG and energy levels of PdAP increased when it is reduced by VC. The increase of BG and energy levels are ascribed to the bad conjugacy of reduced PdAP. What is more, the result of situ UV–vis is consistent with this theoretical result.

Thus, the mechanism of this switch system can be concluded as follows (Scheme 2 ): firstly, the Hb is adsorbed on PdAP NPs. As a fluorescent sensing platform, PdAP increase the PL intensity of Hb through FRET procedure. Then, when VC is added in this system, the PdAP is reduced by Hb-catalyzed reduction. The phenazine ring is collapsed and the conjugated structure in reduced PdAP is also collapsed which led to the FRET procedure receded. The fluorescence quenched and the operating state is “off” state. Finally, when H2O2 is added in this system, the reduced PdAP is oxidized again. The phenazine rings form again which limited the rotation of pyridine ring and improve the rigidity of PdAP. The PL intensity increase and the operating state is “on”.

In summary, a simple, novel, stable, sensitive, and selective oxidation–reduction fluorescent switch was formed by PdAP NPs. As a fluorescent sensing platform, PdAP increased the PL intensity Hb through FRET procedure between the π-conjugation systems of PdAP and Hb. The operating state depends on the oxidized state of PdAP. When the PdAP was reduced by VC in Hb-catalyzed reduction progress, the operating state is “off”. The operating state is “on” when the PdAP is oxidized by H2O2. The experimental results are consistent with the theoretical result. This switch system has high signal–noise ratio and can be used as a sensitive and selective probe for VC and H2O2. This work is helpful to construct fluorescent switch systems with high stability.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by China West Normal University Fund (12B017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.synthmet.2015.03.028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
